main_summary.summary: Clarence Magee, born May 1932 in Columbia, Marion County, Mississippi, was interviewed December 1, 2015 by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop and Guha Shankar at the University of Southern Mississippi's McCain Library. Magee recounts growing up the oldest of ten siblings on a sharecropping farm his father later purchased, attending segregated schools that lacked indoor plumbing and used hand-me-down books, and earning a degree at Alcorn A&M College (now Alcorn State University). After two years of Army service including a posting at Fort Eustis, Virginia, and orders to Germany, he settled in Hattiesburg, where he taught at Lillie Burney School, Rowan High School, Wiggins, and Prentiss Institute before joining the federal Department of Agriculture Food and Consumer Service. Magee describes his repeated attempts to register to vote under the Forrest County circuit clerk Theron Lynd, his and his wife Carey Bradley McGee's role as plaintiffs in U.S. v. Lynd prosecuted by John Doar, joining the NAACP under presidents J.C. Fairley and Dr. C.E. Smith, hosting Freedom Summer volunteers, serving on the biracial education committee with Superintendent Dr. Spinks and Earline Boas, his 1962 subpoena to Aberdeen with his wife and T.F. Boyne to verify Clyde Kennard's survival at Parchman Farm, and standing watch at the Vernon Dahmer site after the 1966 firebombing.
key_themes: Voter registration under Forrest County clerk Theron Lynd
Segregated education and the value of schooling
main_summary.historical_significance:
Magee's testimony anchors the Forrest County voter-suppression record from the plaintiff's chair: he describes the multi-year cat-and-mouse pattern of the registrar refusing applications without explanation, his wife Carey Bradley McGee writing 'Hattiesburg' as her precinct when none was indicated, and federal prosecutor John Doar using her application alongside a white truck driver's at the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals. The interview also preserves a first-person witness account of the December 1962 Aberdeen habeas corpus hearing for Clyde Kennard, naming attorney R. Jess Brown and identifying T.F. Boyne as the third subpoenaed witness, and a community-watch role at the Vernon Dahmer property after the January 1966 Klan firebombing.

As a teacher protected by Hattiesburg principal N.R. Burger's policy of shielding city teachers from NAACP-retaliation firings that hit county teachers, Magee illustrates the structural difference between municipal and county school employment during the movement era. His later work with the Forrest County community action agency, his correspondence with Governor William Waller protesting a Dahmer-killer pardon, and his testimony about the locally named 'deep guns' armed-defense watch (distinct from the Deacons for Defense) all contribute primary-source detail to the Hattiesburg organizing record.

chapters.title: Marion County Sharecropping Childhood
chapters.summary: Magee describes being born May 1932 in Columbia, Marion County, Mississippi, the oldest of ten siblings. His father was a sharecropper who vowed his children would attend school and eventually purchased four acres. The family raised chickens, cows, hogs, and corn, drew water from a hillside spring, and studied by firelight without electricity or indoor plumbing.
related_events: Family migration from sharecropping to land ownership

chapters.title: Family, Discipline, and Cotton
chapters.summary: Magee recalls his mother's death during childbirth thirty days into his Alcorn semester, his father's diabetic coma the following month, and his grandmother raising the younger siblings. He describes his father as a 'trainer' who paid the children a penny per pound of cotton picked, taught them to weigh their own loads, and motivated him through a scolding that led Magee to outpick his father from that day forward.

chapters.title: Segregated Schools and Alcorn College
chapters.summary: Magee contrasts his walk to a homemade bus owned by his uncle with the white students' real bus that drove past his house, and recalls books with previous students' names written inside them. He attended Alcorn A&M College (now Alcorn State University) starting in 1950, worked through PES control and the model home program to pay room and board, and received the nickname KOTAKT for carrying garbage from the female dormitory.

chapters.title: Army Service and Move to Hattiesburg
chapters.summary: Magee was drafted after his deferred Alcorn graduation, served two years including a posting at Fort Eustis, Virginia, and was sent to Germany for fourteen months. He married Carey Bradley while in service. After discharge in 1956 he taught at Wiggins for one year, Prentiss Institute for two years coaching the college basketball team, then elementary grades in Hattiesburg before joining Lillie Burney School and later Rowan High School in 1969.

chapters.title: Voter Registration and U.S. v. Lynd
chapters.summary: After purchasing his Hattiesburg home in 1956, Magee began paying the two-dollar poll tax and attempting to register with the Forrest County circuit clerk. He recounts being called 'boy' by registrar Theron Lynd, being told the registration was 'not quite satisfactory' without explanation, and his wife Carey Bradley McGee taking the test three times. He was subpoenaed when the federal Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals case proceeded, with John Doar prosecuting using his wife's application against that of an illiterate white truck driver who passed.
related_events: U.S. v. Lynd federal voter registration case
Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals proceedings

chapters.title: NAACP and Freedom Summer Support
chapters.summary: Magee joined the NAACP through his church St. James CME, where branch president J.C. Fairley was a member. After Freedom Summer began, his church provided housing and meals for incoming volunteers, and Magee conducted sensitivity training preparing Northern students for Mississippi conditions, including advising against calling police 'pigs.' He worked closely with Dr. C.E. Smith, who succeeded Fairley as branch president, and served on the NAACP education committee.

chapters.title: Biracial Education Committee
chapters.summary: Magee served on the biracial committee working with the Hattiesburg school district on desegregation strategy. He describes the committee's pre-meeting role-play preparations, witnessing Earline Boas (NAACP education committee chair) confront D.R. Patrick when he pointed his finger in her face, and meetings with Superintendent Dr. Spinks. Magee says the immediate goal was better supplies and equipment rather than 'rubbing shoulders with other races.'

chapters.title: Clyde Kennard and Vernon Dahmer
chapters.summary: In December 1962, Magee, his wife, and T.F. Boyne were subpoenaed to Aberdeen, Mississippi to witness that Clyde Kennard was still alive at Parchman Farm, with attorney R. Jess Brown representing the case. Magee knew Vernon Dahmer through his two sons who were Alcorn classmates. After the January 1966 firebombing, Magee and Charles Phillip drove out at night to stand watch at the Dahmer property. He wrote a letter to Governor William Waller protesting the pardon of one of Dahmer's killers, and was present for the eventual Sam Bowers conviction.

chapters.title: Education, Legacy, and Community Policing
chapters.summary: Magee identifies education three times as the answer to the country's ills, and credits N.R. Burger, principal of the city school, with shielding city teachers from the NAACP-retaliation firings that hit county teachers. He describes his current work with the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement on the Pinebelt complex housing project that cost $121 million, the Forrest County community action agency for Head Start, and ongoing community policing dialogue between NAACP, clergy, judges, and the University of Southern Mississippi to prevent a Ferguson, Missouri-style incident.
related_events: Forrest County community action agency formation

chapters.title: Great-Uncle's Lynching and Closing
chapters.summary: Magee shares that his great-uncle was lynched on Richburg Road in Forrest County in 1929, near where the Pinebelt complex stands today off Hardy Street and Broad Street. No one was ever brought to trial. Magee learned of it after moving to the area, has spoken with one researcher about it, and reflects that 'just like it was them, it could have been me.'

loc_video.caption: Clarence Magee oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, 2015 December 01
